During the interactive process, PRC will consider, for example, the following potential accommodations to the extent applicable: (1) making existing facilities accessible; (2) job restructuring; (3) providing part-time or modified work schedules; (4) placing the Team Member on a leave of absence; (5) acquiring or modifying equipment; (6) changing tests, training materials, or policies; (7) providing qualified readers or interpreters; and (8) reassigning the Team Member to a vacant position for which the Team Member is qualified. Reassignment as a form of reasonable accommodation under this policy will be to a vacant position, if available, with equivalent pay, status, and other relevant factors ( e.g ., benefits, geographical location) or, if no such equivalent position is vacant, the Team Member will be offered the closest vacant lower level position that is available. Reassignment does not include promoting the Team Member.

e.g ., benefits, geographical location) or, if no such equivalent position is vacant, the Team Member will be offered the closest vacant lower level position that is available. Reassignment does not include promoting the Team Member. Team Members must compete for any vacant position that would constitute a promotion. Implementation. If a reasonable accommodation is provided under this policy, Human Resources will work with the Team Member or applicant, the supervisor, and other individuals as appropriate to oversee implementation of the accommodation. NO RETALIATION PRC will not retaliate against any Team Member or applicant who requests an accommodation for a disability. If Team Members or applicants are subject to or witness retaliatory conduct that violates this policy, they should immediately report the conduct to Human Resources. CONFIDENTIALITY OF MEDICAL RECORDS

PRC will not retaliate against any Team Member or applicant who requests an accommodation for a disability. If Team Members or applicants are subject to or witness retaliatory conduct that violates this policy, they should immediately report the conduct to Human Resources. CONFIDENTIALITY OF MEDICAL RECORDS All PRC staff engaged in the interactive process will keep all disability-related information, including medical information, confidential and will not disclose such information to others, except as required by applicable law or as directed by legal counsel for PRC. Medical information (including medical certification forms) will be kept in a confidential medical file, separate from the Team Member’s personnel file.
Original Issue Date :		June 5, 2009 Title :				Appeal Process Affected Departments : 		PRC_ALL

Policy and Purpose :	To provide a process for eligible Team Members to appeal tangible employment actions that materially and adversely impact their employment status, terms, conditions, privileges, opportunities, or compensation ( e.g ., demotions, involuntary separations for cause, and other similar material actions); and to appeal certain final determinations regarding complaints of discrimination, harassment, or retaliation. Repeals and Replaces: Appeal Process for Involuntary Separations (revised 1/1/2017) A.	Policy Any Team Member from the entry level to the Director level who successfully completes the applicable introductory period, and thereafter suffers a tangible employment action that materially and adversely impacts his/her employment status, terms, conditions, privileges, opportunities, or compensation ( e.g ., demotions, involuntary separations for cause, and other similar material actions), may appeal that employment action under this policy. For purposes of this policy, “involuntary separation for cause” does not include group layoffs or voluntary resignations.

e.g ., demotions, involuntary separations for cause, and other similar material actions), may appeal that employment action under this policy. For purposes of this policy, “involuntary separation for cause” does not include group layoffs or voluntary resignations. In addition, any Team Member who makes an internal complaint of discrimination, harassment, or retaliation may appeal PRC’s final determination regarding that complaint if the Team Member has substantial evidence that: (1) procedural error(s) substantially affected the fairness of the complaint resolution process such that the final determination would have been different if the procedural error(s) did not occur; or (2) PRC’s final determination is clearly contrary to the evidence presented during the investigation of the complaint such that a reasonable person could not have reached the final determination based on the evidence presented. B.	Process 1.	Filing an Appeal.

In addition, any Team Member who makes an internal complaint of discrimination, harassment, or retaliation may appeal PRC’s final determination regarding that complaint if the Team Member has substantial evidence that: (1) procedural error(s) substantially affected the fairness of the complaint resolution process such that the final determination would have been different if the procedural error(s) did not occur; or (2) PRC’s final determination is clearly contrary to the evidence presented during the investigation of the complaint such that a reasonable person could not have reached the final determination based on the evidence presented. B.	Process 1.	Filing an Appeal. To start the appeal process, the Team Member must submit the attached “Appeal Form” to the Human Resources Department. The Appeal Form must be delivered to the Human Resources Department via e-mail, fax, hand delivery, U.S. mail, or overnight delivery ( e.g ., FedEx, UPS, etc.)

The Appeal Form must be delivered to the Human Resources Department via e-mail, fax, hand delivery, U.S. mail, or overnight delivery ( e.g ., FedEx, UPS, etc.) within 15 calendar days of the employment action or final determination . NO LATE-FILED APPEALS WILL BE CONSIDERED . If an Appeal is rejected as late-filed, and the Team Member wishes to challenge that determination, the Team Member will be responsible for proving that the Appeal Form was delivered to the Human Resources Department by the applicable deadline. 2.	Notice of Hearing the Appeal. Within seven (7) calendar days of the date the Appeal Form is delivered to the Human Resources Department, the Human Resources Department will provide a written Notice of Hearing to the appealing Team Member and the Vice President of the Team Member’s Department.

If an Appeal is rejected as late-filed, and the Team Member wishes to challenge that determination, the Team Member will be responsible for proving that the Appeal Form was delivered to the Human Resources Department by the applicable deadline. 2.	Notice of Hearing the Appeal. Within seven (7) calendar days of the date the Appeal Form is delivered to the Human Resources Department, the Human Resources Department will provide a written Notice of Hearing to the appealing Team Member and the Vice President of the Team Member’s Department. The Notice of Hearing will include the date, time, and place of the hearing, and the name of the external third party neutral assigned to hear the Appeal (“Neutral”). The Notice of Hearing will be provided at least 15 calendar days in advance of the hearing date. 3.	Procedures for Hearing the Appeal. a.	Attendance.

The Notice of Hearing will be provided at least 15 calendar days in advance of the hearing date. 3.	Procedures for Hearing the Appeal. a.	Attendance. Absent extraordinary circumstances, if an appealing Team Member fails to appear for the hearing, the Appeal will be dismissed with prejudice. b.	Direct Representation Only. The appealing Team Member and the Team Member’s Department will represent themselves at the hearing. No third party representatives will be permitted, except where the appealing Team Member is permanently medically incapacitated and physically unable to participate in a hearing, regardless of when it is scheduled ( e.g ., bedridden with a terminal illness), and the incapacitation is confirmed by proper medical certification. c.	Interpreters and Other Accommodations. If necessary, the Neutral may designate language interpreters or make medically necessary accommodations. All requests for interpreters and medically necessary accommodations must be in writing.

c.	Interpreters and Other Accommodations. If necessary, the Neutral may designate language interpreters or make medically necessary accommodations. All requests for interpreters and medically necessary accommodations must be in writing. Requests for medically necessary accommodations must also be accompanied by proper medical certification demonstrating the need for accommodation. d.	Evidence. The Team Member and the Department may present, and the Neutral will receive, any and all documents and witness testimony directly related to the facts of the Appeal. The parties must submit all of their documentary evidence and their final witness lists to the Neutral no later than five (5) calendar days prior to the hearing date . By filing the Appeal, the appealing Team Member waives any and all rights to privacy and confidentiality as to any and all documents in PRC’s possession, custody, or control that are relevant to the Appeal. e.	Order of Presentations. i.

By filing the Appeal, the appealing Team Member waives any and all rights to privacy and confidentiality as to any and all documents in PRC’s possession, custody, or control that are relevant to the Appeal. e.	Order of Presentations. i. The appealing Team Member will begin by providing an opening statement explaining the basis for the Appeal. After the opening statement, the Team Member may present his/her evidence, if any. ii. After the appealing Team Member presents his/her opening statement and evidence, if any, the relevant Department will provide a statement explaining the basis for the challenged employment action or final determination. After the relevant Department presents its statement, it may present its evidence, if any. f.	Questioning Witnesses. The parties and the Neutral may question any witness who provides testimony during the hearing. The Neutral will control the mode and order of questioning witnesses.

f.	Questioning Witnesses. The parties and the Neutral may question any witness who provides testimony during the hearing. The Neutral will control the mode and order of questioning witnesses. Questions to witnesses must be clear, relevant, and directed at specific facts. Questions to witnesses may not be used to annoy, harass, embarrass, or otherwise intimidate the witness. g.	Viewing Evidence. A case file containing all documentary evidence and witness lists submitted by the parties to the Neutral will be made available for viewing in the Human Resources Department two (2) hours prior to the start time of the hearing. The parties may not photograph, copy, or otherwise duplicate any portion of the case file; and the parties may not remove any portion of the case file from the Human Resources Department. The Neutral will collect all case files in the possession of the parties at the conclusion of the hearing.

A case file containing all documentary evidence and witness lists submitted by the parties to the Neutral will be made available for viewing in the Human Resources Department two (2) hours prior to the start time of the hearing. The parties may not photograph, copy, or otherwise duplicate any portion of the case file; and the parties may not remove any portion of the case file from the Human Resources Department. The Neutral will collect all case files in the possession of the parties at the conclusion of the hearing. h.	Timing. Hearings will generally be limited to four (4) hours. The Neutral may, in his/her sole discretion, permit additional time for a hearing upon a showing of good cause. 4.	Decision of the Neutral.

Hearings will generally be limited to four (4) hours. The Neutral may, in his/her sole discretion, permit additional time for a hearing upon a showing of good cause. 4.	Decision of the Neutral. Within 7 calendar days of the hearing, the Neutral will complete a review of all statements and evidence presented during the hearing and issue a written decision as follows: a. For Appeals challenging a tangible employment action that materially and adversely impacts the appealing Team Member’s employment status, terms, conditions, privileges, opportunities, or compensation, the Neutral will determine whether the evidence establishes it was more likely than not the employment action was taken for a fair business reason . If the answer is yes, the Neutral will issue a decision upholding the employment action. If the answer is no, the Neutral will issue a decision rescinding the employment action. b.

If the answer is yes, the Neutral will issue a decision upholding the employment action. If the answer is no, the Neutral will issue a decision rescinding the employment action. b. For Appeals challenging a final determination on a complaint of discrimination, harassment, or retaliation on the grounds that procedural error substantially affected the fairness of the resolution process, the Neutral will determine whether the evidence establishes it was more likely than not the final determination would have been the same regardless of the alleged procedural error . If the answer is yes, the Neutral will issue a decision upholding the final determination. If the answer is no, the Neutral will issue a decision vacating the final determination and directing PRC to reprocess the complaint consistent with the applicable complaint resolution procedures. c.

If the answer is yes, the Neutral will issue a decision upholding the final determination. If the answer is no, the Neutral will issue a decision vacating the final determination and directing PRC to reprocess the complaint consistent with the applicable complaint resolution procedures. c. For Appeals alleging PRC’s final determination regarding a complaint of discrimination, harassment, or retaliation is clearly contrary to the evidence presented during the investigation of the complaint, the Neutral will determine whether a reasonable person could have reached the final determination based on the evidence presented . If the answer is yes, the Neutral will issue a decision upholding the final determination. If the answer is no, the Neutral will issue a decision vacating the final determination and directing PRC to issue a new final determination reasonably supported by the evidence presented. d.

If the answer is yes, the Neutral will issue a decision upholding the final determination. If the answer is no, the Neutral will issue a decision vacating the final determination and directing PRC to issue a new final determination reasonably supported by the evidence presented. d. The Neutral’s decision will be binding on the parties and the final step of the Appeal Process as specified in this Policy. 5.	Notice of Decision. Notice of the Neutral’s decision will be mailed to all parties via certified mail, return receipt requested, within 10 calendar days of the hearing. The Notice will contain a full copy of the Neutral’s Decision; detailed information about the appealing Team Member’s reinstatement, if any; and a copy of the Pechanga Employment Claims Act. 6.	Procedures for Rescinding an Employment Action.

Notice of the Neutral’s decision will be mailed to all parties via certified mail, return receipt requested, within 10 calendar days of the hearing. The Notice will contain a full copy of the Neutral’s Decision; detailed information about the appealing Team Member’s reinstatement, if any; and a copy of the Pechanga Employment Claims Act. 6.	Procedures for Rescinding an Employment Action. If the Neutral’s decision rescinds a demotion or separation, the appealing Team Member will be reinstated with seniority and back pay to the date of the rescinded employment action. If the appealing Team Member’s Department determines some level of discipline is still warranted based on the findings of the Neutral, the Department shall consult with the Vice President of Human Resources (or his/her designee) and designated legal counsel to determine the appropriate level of alternative discipline, if any.

text: Policy and Purpose: Banishment from Pechanga Resort & Casino (PR&C) property. All Team Members who have been involuntarily terminated and/or on Suspension Pending Investigation. Procedures: PR&C may deny access to the property to involuntarily terminated and/or suspended Team Member(s) who: is involuntarily terminated, and who does not have lawful business to pursue at PR&C or; are engaged in unlawful off duty misconduct including but not limited to, criminal harassment, drug possession or sale, stalking, vandalism, domestic violence; threaten health, safety, and welfare of PR&C Team Member(s); those ordered to remain away from a Team Member(s); Notifying Involuntary Terminated Team Members The Department of Public Safety Designee shall notify the Team Member of the ban at the time of the suspension and/or termination meeting. This notice shall: state the factors, or combination of factors, justifying restriction cite the rule and/or policy, and include case number
source: PRC ALL Banishment from PRC Property.pdf

text: This notice shall: state the factors, or combination of factors, justifying restriction cite the rule and/or policy, and include case number notify Team Member that, failure to adhere to the property restriction notice may result in criminal trespassing charges. be mailed a copy of the restriction notice, return receipt requested if the Team Member is not present at the meeting. Please refer to Restriction Notice RN-1300-05 Banning of Involuntarily Terminated Team Members Banning of terminated Team Member(s) shall be determined and approved by the Vice President of Public Safety, Vice President of Human Resources/Talent Management or the General Manager. text: Original Issue Date :	08/17/2010 SOP Title :	Benefits: PTO Donation Program Policy Affected Departments : 	All Full-Time Team Members
source: Benefits PTO Donation Program Policy May 2019.docx

text: Policy and Purpose :	To standardize the administration of the PTO Leave Sharing Program and ensure fairness and consistency throughout Pechanga Resort Casino (Please type your change comments or explanations of the new policy below) This policy repeals and replaces the following policy. PTO Leave Donation Program, #11398 Pechanga Resort Casino (PRC) permits Team Members to voluntarily donate portions of their earned Paid Time Off (PTO) hours to other Team Members who are suffering from a serious illness or injury, or that of an immediate family member, including time needed for bereavement, which necessitates a prolonged absence from work. For purpose of this policy, an immediate family member is defined as a spouse, child or parent. This policy is applicable to all Full-Time Team Members and shall be free of all management solicitation. Eligibility for Recipient
source: Benefits PTO Donation Program Policy May 2019.docx

text: For purpose of this policy, an immediate family member is defined as a spouse, child or parent. This policy is applicable to all Full-Time Team Members and shall be free of all management solicitation. Eligibility for Recipient The recipient must have completed at least one (1) year of continuous service with PRC. The recipient or recipient’s immediate family member must be suffering from a catastrophic / critical illness or injury which necessitates the Team Member's prolonged absence from work, and for which the Team member has no PTO time available. The recipient cannot be at a Director level or above within PRC. The recipient must have exhausted all accrued PTO time or have an approved intermittent agreement on file. The recipient may receive days from more than one donor but may not receive a total of more than 180 hours.
source: Benefits PTO Donation Program Policy May 2019.docx

text: The recipient cannot be at a Director level or above within PRC. The recipient must have exhausted all accrued PTO time or have an approved intermittent agreement on file. The recipient may receive days from more than one donor but may not receive a total of more than 180 hours. The donated time-off cannot be received on a previously concluded leave. A Team Member who receives donated PTO time is not eligible to receive any cash out of PTO time for twelve (12) months following the receipt of the donation. Eligibility for Donor A Team Member may donate up to a maximum of 180 hours to one recipient. Donated leave must be made in no less than two (2) hour increments. The donor may not deplete their own accrued PTO to less than 40 hours. A donor may not
source: Benefits PTO Donation Program Policy May 2019.docx

text: The donor may not deplete their own accrued PTO to less than 40 hours. A donor may not revoke the time-off donation once the donation has been transmitted to the recipient by Payroll. Conditions The donor's PTO will be reduced by the number of hours which are donated. The Program does not alter existing policies regarding entitlement and/or accrual of PTO. The Program does not alter any current leave of absence policy or requirements under current policies. The Program does not provide for any additional return to work guarantees outside existing leave of absence policies. Procedures A Team Member may participate in this program as a recipient or donor by contacting the Benefits Department. A Supervisor/Manager, or Team Member wishing to donate time, may also initiate the process on behalf of the Team Member by contacting the Benefits Department.
source: Benefits PTO Donation Program Policy May 2019.docx

text: Procedures A Team Member may participate in this program as a recipient or donor by contacting the Benefits Department. A Supervisor/Manager, or Team Member wishing to donate time, may also initiate the process on behalf of the Team Member by contacting the Benefits Department. Once a recipient is approved for the program the Benefits Department will communicate to the recipient, donor, and/or supervisor as appropriate. The donor and the recipient will fill out the required forms. No one shall directly or indirectly intimidate, threaten or coerce, or attempt to intimidate, threaten or coerce any other Team Member for the purpose of interfering with any right which such Team Member may have with respect to contributing, receiving or using paid time-off under this program.
source: Benefits PTO Donation Program Policy May 2019.docx

text: Once a recipient is approved for the program the Benefits Department will communicate to the recipient, donor, and/or supervisor as appropriate. The donor and the recipient will fill out the required forms. No one shall directly or indirectly intimidate, threaten or coerce, or attempt to intimidate, threaten or coerce any other Team Member for the purpose of interfering with any right which such Team Member may have with respect to contributing, receiving or using paid time-off under this program. This shall include promising to confer or conferring any benefit (such as appointment, promotion, or compensation) or effecting or threatening to effect any reprisal (such as deprivation of an appointment, promotion, or compensation). Any Team Member who engages in the above prohibited conduct may be subject to disciplinary action.
source: Benefits PTO Donation Program Policy May 2019.docx

text: PECHANGA BAND OF LUISEÑO INDIANS CODE OF ORDINANCES TITLE 10 TRIBAL LABOR AND EMPLOYMENT CODE DIVISION 2 EMPLOYMENT RIGHTS ARTICLE 1 PECHANGA EMPLOYMENT CLAIMS ACT OF 2018 Title. This Act shall be known and cited as the Pechanga Employment Claims Act of 2018 (Act"), Pechanga Code of Ordinances Title 10, Division 2, Article 1. 1.2 Authority & Enactment. This Act was enacted by the Pechanga General Membership, the authorized governing body of the Pechanga Band of Luiseño Indians, as evidenced by General Membership Resolution No. 180415-02. Purpose. The purpose of this Act is to prohibit Discrimination, Harassment, and Retaliation by Employers against Gaming Employees and Gaming Applicants; and to establish a claims process for resolving such Discrimination, Harassment, and Retaliation. 1.4 Definitions. As used in this Act, the capitalized terms set forth below shall have the following meanings unless the context requires otherwise: (a) Administrative Law Judge.
source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

text: 1.4 Definitions. As used in this Act, the capitalized terms set forth below shall have the following meanings unless the context requires otherwise: (a) Administrative Law Judge. Administrative Law Judge means the Administrative Law Judge assigned to perform an administrative review of a Claimant's Claim. (b) Band. Band means the Pechanga Band of Luiseño Indians, sometimes referred to as the Temecula Band of Luiseño Indians, federally recognized as the Pechanga Band of Luiseño Mission Indians of the Pechanga Reservation. (c) Claim. Claim means a claim brought pursuant to this Act by a Gaming Employee or Gaming Applicant. (d) Claimant. Claimant means a Gaming Employee or Gaming Applicant who submits a Claim pursuant to this Act. (e) Tribal-State Compact.
source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

text: (d) Claimant. Claimant means a Gaming Employee or Gaming Applicant who submits a Claim pursuant to this Act. (e) Tribal-State Compact. Tribal-State Compact means the Tribal-State Compact between the State of California and the Pechanga Band of Luiseño Indians, initially enacted on or about September 10, 1999, as amended on August 28, 2006 and August 4, 2016, and as published in the Federal Register. Discrimination. Discrimination means taking a tangible employment action that materially and adversely impacts the employment status, terms, conditions, privileges, opportunities, or compensation of a Gaming Employee or Gaming Applicant based on the Gaming Employee's or Gaming Applicant's actual or perceived Protected Characteristic, where the employment action would not have occurred but for the actual or perceived Protected Characteristic. (g) Employer. Employer means the entity responsible for hiring, firing, and controlling the work of a Gaming Employee or Gaming Applicant.
source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

text: employment action would not have occurred but for the actual or perceived Protected Characteristic. (g) Employer. Employer means the entity responsible for hiring, firing, and controlling the work of a Gaming Employee or Gaming Applicant. The term Employer includes an agent, proxy, or alter ego acting on the entity's behalf. (h) Gaming Activities. Gaming Activities means the Class III gaming activities authorized under the Compact. (i) Gaming Applicant. Gaming Applicant means an individual applying for employment in, in connection with, or relating to the operation of, the Band's Gaming Operation, Gaming Facility, or Gaming Activities. (i) Gaming Employee.
source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

text: (i) Gaming Applicant. Gaming Applicant means an individual applying for employment in, in connection with, or relating to the operation of, the Band's Gaming Operation, Gaming Facility, or Gaming Activities. (i) Gaming Employee. Gaming Employee means an individual employed in, in connection with, or relating to the operation of, the Band's Gaming Operation, Gaming Facility, or Gaming Activities; who is subjected to the Employer's control and direction; and who receives a salary or hourly wage directly from the Employer for services rendered in, in connection with, or relating to the operation of, the Band's Gaming Operation, Gaming Facility, or Gaming Activities. Independent contractors are not considered Gaming Employees. (k) Gaming Facility.
source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

text: Gaming Employee means an individual employed in, in connection with, or relating to the operation of, the Band's Gaming Operation, Gaming Facility, or Gaming Activities; who is subjected to the Employer's control and direction; and who receives a salary or hourly wage directly from the Employer for services rendered in, in connection with, or relating to the operation of, the Band's Gaming Operation, Gaming Facility, or Gaming Activities. Independent contractors are not considered Gaming Employees. (k) Gaming Facility. Gaming Facility means any building in which Gaming Activities or any Gaming Operations occur, or in which business records, receipts, or funds of the Gaming Operation are maintained (excluding offsite facilities primarily dedicated to storage of those records and financial institutions), and all rooms, buildings, and areas, including hotels, parking lots, and walkways, a principal purpose of which is to serve the activities of the Gaming Operation and Facility rather than providing them with an incidental benefit.

text: Independent contractors are not considered Gaming Employees. (k) Gaming Facility. Gaming Operation. Gaming Operation means the business enterprise that offers and operates Gaming Activities, whether exclusively or otherwise. (m) Harassment.
source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

text: Gaming Operation. Gaming Operation means the business enterprise that offers and operates Gaming Activities, whether exclusively or otherwise. (m) Harassment. Harassment means unwelcome conduct in the workplace that is based on a Gaming Applicant's or Gaming Employee's actual or perceived Protected Characteristic, is sufficiently severe or pervasive to alter the terms and conditions of employment or unreasonably interfere with a Gaming Applicant's or Gaming Employee's performance, and would be considered threatening, intimidating, hostile, or abusive by a reasonable person. (n) Mediator. Mediator means the third-party neutral assigned to mediate the Claimant's Claim. (o) Pechanga Tribal Court. Pechanga Tribal Court means the court established by Section 103.1 of the Pechanga Band of Luiseño Mission Indians Judicial Ordinance. (p) Protected Activity.
source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

text: (o) Pechanga Tribal Court. Pechanga Tribal Court means the court established by Section 103.1 of the Pechanga Band of Luiseño Mission Indians Judicial Ordinance. (p) Protected Activity. Protected Activity means opposing practices prohibited by this Act; filing a Claim; testifying, assisting, or participating in an investigation, proceeding, or hearing conducted pursuant to this Act; exercising leave rights granted by applicable Employer policies; making a good-faith report of illegal activity or violations of the Employer's policies; or testifying, assisting, or participating in an investigation into illegal activity or violations of the Employer's policies. Protected Characteristic. Protected Characteristic means a Gaming Applicant's or Gaming Employee's race, color, religion, sex, gender, national origin, ancestry, physical or mental disability or medical condition, age for individuals over 40 years of age, genetic information, sexual orientation, gender identity, gender expression, marital status, or veteran or military status. (r) Retaliation.
source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

text: Protected Characteristic. Protected Characteristic means a Gaming Applicant's or Gaming Employee's race, color, religion, sex, gender, national origin, ancestry, physical or mental disability or medical condition, age for individuals over 40 years of age, genetic information, sexual orientation, gender identity, gender expression, marital status, or veteran or military status. (r) Retaliation. Retaliation means taking a tangible employment action that materially and adversely impacts the employment status, terms, conditions, privileges, opportunities, or compensation of a Gaming Applicant or Gaming Employee based on the Gaming Applicant's or Gaming Employee's Protected Activity, where the employment action would not have occurred but for the Gaming Applicant's or Gaming Employee's Protected Activity. (s) Tribal Council. Tribal Council means the Tribal Council of the Pechanga Band of Luiseño Indians.
source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

(s) Tribal Council. Tribal Council means the Tribal Council of the Pechanga Band of Luiseño Indians. source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

text: 1.5 Prohibited Conduct. Discrimination, Harassment, or Retaliation by an Employer against a Gaming Employee or Gaming Applicant is prohibited. For purposes of this Section 1.5, an action shall be deemed to be "by an Employer" if it is: (a) A tangible employment action that materially and adversely impacts the employment status, terms, conditions, privileges, opportunities, or compensation of the subject Gaming Employee or Gaming Applicant; (b) Harassment by a supervisor with immediate or successively higher authority over the Gaming Employee or Gaming Applicant, except that an Employer may not be held liable for such Harassment if the Employer exercised reasonable care to prevent and promptly correct the Harassment, and the Gaming Employee or Gaming Applicant unreasonably failed to take advantage of the preventive or corrective opportunities provided by the Employer or to otherwise avoid harm;

(a) General Provisions. The Claims Resolution Process set forth in this Section 1.8 is the exclusive legal remedy for Discrimination, Harassment, or Retaliation by an Employer against a Gaming Applicant or Gaming Employee.
source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

text: 1.8 Claims Resolution Process. (a) General Provisions. The Claims Resolution Process set forth in this Section 1.8 is the exclusive legal remedy for Discrimination, Harassment, or Retaliation by an Employer against a Gaming Applicant or Gaming Employee. The Band expressly rejects and will not recognize any alternative legal remedy for such Discrimination, Harassment, or Retaliation. An individual may only file a Claim under this Act if the individual was a Gaming Applicant or Gaming Employee at the time of the wrongful conduct alleged in the Claim. Any Claim submitted by an individual who was not a Gaming Applicant or Gaming Employee at the time of the wrongful conduct alleged in the Claim is procedurally barred and will be rejected. Claims may only be brought against Employers of Gaming Applicants and Gaming Employees.
source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

text: An individual may only file a Claim under this Act if the individual was a Gaming Applicant or Gaming Employee at the time of the wrongful conduct alleged in the Claim. Any Claim submitted by an individual who was not a Gaming Applicant or Gaming Employee at the time of the wrongful conduct alleged in the Claim is procedurally barred and will be rejected. Claims may only be brought against Employers of Gaming Applicants and Gaming Employees. Claims may not be brought against individual employees or any entity not defined as an Employer under this Act. Claims filed under this Act may only be pursued on an individual basis. Class, collective, and representative claims are prohibited. The result of the Claims Resolution Process set forth in this Section 1.8 shall be final and binding on all parties to the Claim.
source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

text: Claims filed under this Act may only be pursued on an individual basis. Class, collective, and representative claims are prohibited. The result of the Claims Resolution Process set forth in this Section 1.8 shall be final and binding on all parties to the Claim. A Claim may not be submitted for resolution more than once. The procedural requirements set forth in this Section 1.8 are jurisdictional. Neither the Mediator, Administrative Law Judge, nor Pechanga Tribal Court is authorized to waive any procedural requirement of this Section 1.8. For purposes of this Act, the date of mailing of any document shall be the date such document is deposited with the United States Postal Service or an overnight delivery service, as evidenced by a postmark or equivalent marking confirming the date of receipt by the United States Postal Service or overnight delivery service. (b) Administrative Procedure.
source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

text: For purposes of this Act, the date of mailing of any document shall be the date such document is deposited with the United States Postal Service or an overnight delivery service, as evidenced by a postmark or equivalent marking confirming the date of receipt by the United States Postal Service or overnight delivery service. (b) Administrative Procedure. In order to pursue a Claim for Discrimination, Harassment, or Retaliation under this Act, a Claimant must give the Clerk of the Pechanga Tribal Court written notice of the Claimant's intent to pursue a Claim within one (1) year of the alleged act of Discrimination, Harassment, or Retaliation. The written notice required by this subdivision 1.8.(b)(1) shall be submitted either (i) in person; (ii) via overnight delivery; (iii) via certified mail; or (iv) via electronic mail. The written notice shall be submitted to: Clerk Pechanga Tribal Court P.O. Box 1477 Temecula, CA 92593
source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

Box 1477 Temecula, CA 92593 courtclerk@pechanga-nsn.gov Within 30 calendar days of receiving the Claimant's written notice specified in subdivision 1.8(b)(1), the Clerk of the Pechanga Tribal Court shall provide the Claimant, by personal service or certified mail, return receipt requested, an administrative claim form and written notice that the Claimant shall have one hundred eighty (180) calendar days from the date of the Clerk's notice to proceed with the Claim. In order to proceed with the Claim, the Claimant must file the administrative claim form, completed and executed under penalty of perjury, with the Clerk of the Pechanga Tribal Court within one hundred eighty (180) calendar days of the date of the Clerk's written notice specified in subdivision 1.8(b)(2).

source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

text: If the Claimant submits the administrative claim form via certified mail, the date of mailing shall be considered the filing date of the Claim. Within 15 calendar days of the filing date of the Claim, the Clerk of the Pechanga Tribal Court will assign the Claim to a third-party neutral ("Mediator"). Within 30 calendar days of receipt of the Claim, the Mediator will mediate the Claim. If the Claim is resolved through mediation, the Claimant shall submit a request for dismissal of the Claim to the Clerk of the Pechanga Tribal Court no later than the day the resolution becomes final. Upon receipt of the Claimant's request for dismissal of the Claim, the Clerk of the Pechanga Tribal Court shall dismiss the Claim with prejudice.
source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

text: Within 30 calendar days of receipt of the Claim, the Mediator will mediate the Claim. Upon receipt of the Claimant's request for dismissal of the Claim, the Clerk of the Pechanga Tribal Court shall dismiss the Claim with prejudice. If a Claim is not resolved in mediation, the Claim will be assigned to an Administrative Law Judge ("ALJ") selected at random from a pool assembled pursuant to this Act, which is comprised of individuals with at least seven years of experience in litigation or administrative law as a licensed attorney and expertise in tribal and employment law.

source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

text: If a Claim is not resolved in mediation, the Claim will be assigned to an Administrative Law Judge ("ALJ") selected at random from a pool assembled pursuant to this Act, which is comprised of individuals with at least seven years of experience in litigation or administrative law as a licensed attorney and expertise in tribal and employment law. Within 60 calendar days of the ALJ's receipt of a Claim, the ALJ shall schedule an in-person hearing during which the Claimant and Employer may present relevant evidence regarding the Claim. The hearing will be recorded, and each party may obtain a transcript of the hearing at its own expense. Absent extraordinary circumstances, failure of the Claimant to appear for the hearing will be grounds for dismissal of the Claim. Within 30 calendar days of the hearing, the ALJ shall issue a reasoned decision in writing to the Claimant and Employer.
source: Ord 180415-02 Employment Claims Act includes Reso.pdf

The ALJ's decision shall contain detailed findings of fact and conclusions of law, as well as a description of any Award to be made to Claimant. Any Award to Claimant shall be limited to economic damages in an amount not to exceed the Employer's applicable insurance policy limits or $3,000,000, whichever is greater. text: Original Issue Date :	6/18/2009 SOP Title :	Paid Time Off (PTO) Policy Affected Departments : All

Policy and Purpose :	The purpose of this policy is to standardize the process. It is intended to provide PTO for the purpose of rest, relaxation, planned and unplanned interruptions from the workplace, and for the purpose of attending to personal affairs. (Please type your change comments or explanations of the new policy below) This policy repeals and replaces the following policy. PRC ALL Paid Time Off (PTO), #10227, V9 dated 2/14/2017 It has been revised to include: Additional day accrual for 10+ year Team Members PTO accrual and balance for part-time Team Members It has been revised to delete/revise: C.1.a) PTO continues to accrue while a Team Member is on PTO. Payment of unused PTO – deleted a. Eligibility All Full-time and Part-time Team Members are eligible to request the use of accrued PTO once they complete their introductory period.

Payment of unused PTO – deleted a. Eligibility All Full-time and Part-time Team Members are eligible to request the use of accrued PTO once they complete their introductory period. The introductory period is generally the first ninety (90) days of employment starting from the first day of work. During the introductory period, Team Members accrue PTO but cannot use it. Any time taken off in this period will be unpaid. Upon completion of the introductory period, a Team Member may request PTO. Team Members become eligible to use their accrued PTO on the ninety-first (91st) day of employment. In cases where the introductory period is extended, the Team Member will continue to accrue PTO to the initial date of hire. A Team Member that is separated during the introductory period will not receive payment for PTO hours accrued. Use of PTO

In cases where the introductory period is extended, the Team Member will continue to accrue PTO to the initial date of hire. A Team Member that is separated during the introductory period will not receive payment for PTO hours accrued. Use of PTO All Team Members have the responsibility to plan their time off requests around the operating requirements of their positions. Team Members should schedule PTO well in advance to balance the service and staffing requirements of their position. Team Members may request time off up to six (6) months in advance providing they have the hours available. PTO should be requested at least two (2) weeks in advance. The only exception is for emergency circumstances beyond the Team Member’s control.

Team Members may request time off up to six (6) months in advance providing they have the hours available. PTO should be requested at least two (2) weeks in advance. The only exception is for emergency circumstances beyond the Team Member’s control. In the event of illness or emergency preventing the Team Member from requesting PTO in advance, the Team Member must notify their Supervisor/Manager no later than 2 hours before the start of their scheduled shift and explain the need for the unscheduled time off. All requests must be submitted through Kiicha Team Member Self Service and approved by the Team Member’s   Supervisor/Manager. The Team Member’s Supervisor/Manager maintains the discretion to grant or deny PTO that is requested, with or without proper notice, based on business needs. Supervisors/Managers will provide a response to the requests for PTO no later than seven (7) calendar days after the request was made.

All requests must be submitted through Kiicha Team Member Self Service and approved by the Team Member’s   Supervisor/Manager. The Team Member’s Supervisor/Manager maintains the discretion to grant or deny PTO that is requested, with or without proper notice, based on business needs. Supervisors/Managers will provide a response to the requests for PTO no later than seven (7) calendar days after the request was made. Full-time Team Members are required to take 40 hours of PTO within the calendar year, and Part-time Team Members are required to take 20 hours of PTO within the calendar year. Team Members may not opt to take absences without pay in lieu of PTO for purposes of saving accrued PTO. PTO will run concurrent with any leaves of absence including Pechanga Medical Leave PR&C will save at least forty (40) hours of PTO for Team Members on Pechanga Medical Leave

Team Members may not opt to take absences without pay in lieu of PTO for purposes of saving accrued PTO. PTO will run concurrent with any leaves of absence including Pechanga Medical Leave PR&C will save at least forty (40) hours of PTO for Team Members on Pechanga Medical Leave Team Members can elect to use all available hours for leaves of absence. For non-exempt status Team Members, PTO must be used in no less than half hour (1/2) increments and may be used for early outs. When Team Members request an early out, PTO must be used. If Management offers an early out, PTO use is optional. For Exempt status Team Members, PTO must be used in four (4) hour increments if the absence will result in more than half of the workday.

When Team Members request an early out, PTO must be used. If Management offers an early out, PTO use is optional. For Exempt status Team Members, PTO must be used in four (4) hour increments if the absence will result in more than half of the workday. If the absence is less than four (4) hours of absence, no PTO time will need to be used. Team Members who are denied a request for PTO and “call out” anyway, or do not report for work as scheduled, will be subject to disciplinary action up to and including separation. Unapproved time off taken will automatically be deducted from a Team Member’s PTO balance and may lead to disciplinary actions. Unexpected emergencies/absences cannot be used in this situation.

Team Members who are denied a request for PTO and “call out” anyway, or do not report for work as scheduled, will be subject to disciplinary action up to and including separation. Unapproved time off taken will automatically be deducted from a Team Member’s PTO balance and may lead to disciplinary actions. Unexpected emergencies/absences cannot be used in this situation. If a Team Member is absent from work on a scheduled day without approval, the number of scheduled hours will be deducted from their PTO balance. If they do not have a PTO balance, the absence will be unpaid. In either case the absence will be subject to disciplinary action per the Attendance policy. Accrual Full-time and Part-time Team Members accrue PTO based on actual hours worked. PTO continues to accrue while a Team Member is on PTO.

Accrual Full-time and Part-time Team Members accrue PTO based on actual hours worked. PTO continues to accrue while a Team Member is on PTO. PTO does not accrue during any other type of leave, whether paid or unpaid. Team Members who have completed the introductory period accrue PTO in accordance with the following schedule: FULL-TIME Years of service PTO Accrual FT Maximum hours earned per pay period (80) FT Maximum hours earned per year FT Maximum days earned per year 2 nd thru 4 th 5 th thru 9 th PART-TIME Years of service PTO Accrual PT Maximum hours earned per pay period 58) PT Maximum hours earned per year PT Maximum days earned per year 2 nd thru 4 th 5 th thru 9 th

th thru 9 th Team Members who have completed 10 years of service will be granted an additional eight (8) hours of PTO for Full-time Team Members and an additional four (4) hours for Part-time Team Members to be used within 12 months from day the hours are granted. These hours will have a special code to differentiate them from regular PTO. Additionally, once a team member reaches 10 years of service, the team member will accrue PTO at a higher rate to earn an extra day of PTO per year. FULL-TIME PART-TIME Years of service FT Maximum hours earned per year FT Maximum balance permitted per year PT Maximum hours earned per year PT Maximum balance permitted per year 2 nd thru 4 th 5 th – 9 th Balances are reduced by using PTO hours or utilizing the cash out of PTO hours, per the policy below.

– 9 th Balances are reduced by using PTO hours or utilizing the cash out of PTO hours, per the policy below. Hours will not be retroactively accrued and credited to the Team Member’s PTO balance once the Team Member reaches the maximum balance permitted per year unless approved by the Department Supervisor/Manager and Vice President. Cash out of accrued PTO Status Change When a Team Member transitions from an eligible PTO accrual status to a non-eligible PTO accrual status, PTO will be paid through the next normal payroll cycle following the effective date of change. Cash out request A Team Member may request a cash out once in a twelve month rolling period from date of last PTO cash out, provided the following criteria are met:

When a Team Member transitions from an eligible PTO accrual status to a non-eligible PTO accrual status, PTO will be paid through the next normal payroll cycle following the effective date of change. Cash out request A Team Member may request a cash out once in a twelve month rolling period from date of last PTO cash out, provided the following criteria are met: The Team Member must have taken forty (40) hours of PTO if Full-time or twenty (20) hours of PTO if Part-time in the twelve month rolling period. Time off does not have to be consecutive. There must be a remaining balance of 40 hours in the Full-time Team Member’s account and 20 hours in the Part-time Team Member’s account after the cash out request. PTO hours earned from the 10-year service award cannot be cashed out.

Time off does not have to be consecutive. There must be a remaining balance of 40 hours in the Full-time Team Member’s account and 20 hours in the Part-time Team Member’s account after the cash out request. PTO hours earned from the 10-year service award cannot be cashed out. There is no maximum as to how many hours may be cashed out. Payment of unused PTO Upon separation of employment, all Team Members who have successfully completed their introductory period will be paid their accrued and unused PTO. The PTO balance will be paid to the Team Member less any monies owed to Pechanga Resort & Casino. All payments for PTO are treated as taxable income and will be included in either the Team Member’s final payroll check or paid as a separate payroll check.

Upon separation of employment, all Team Members who have successfully completed their introductory period will be paid their accrued and unused PTO. The PTO balance will be paid to the Team Member less any monies owed to Pechanga Resort & Casino. All payments for PTO are treated as taxable income and will be included in either the Team Member’s final payroll check or paid as a separate payroll check. Team Members transferring to a position with an hourly base rate higher or lower than the base rate of their previous position will maintain their current PTO bank of hours and will be paid PTO hours based upon the current rate in effect at the time the hours are taken.
Original Issue Date :	June 13, 2017 SOP Title :	Overtime Affected Departments : 	PRC_ALL

Policy and Purpose :	This Policy sets standards for working and scheduling overtime, establishes overtime rates of pay, and sets forth the policies and procedures for the election and implementation of Alternative Workweek Schedules. Overtime Policy Non-exempt Team Members will be eligible for overtime compensation as described in Sections B and D, below, for hours worked in excess of their regularly scheduled work hours. Non-exempt Team Members may work beyond their regularly scheduled work hours only as permitted or required by their supervisor. Exempt employees are not eligible for overtime compensation—they are expected to work as many hours as necessary to perform the duties of their positions. Overtime Compensation—Standard Work Schedules Pechanga Resort and Casino (“PR&C”) will compensate its non-exempt Team Members on a standard work schedule (“SWS”) for hours worked in excess of their regularly scheduled work hours as described below.

Exempt employees are not eligible for overtime compensation—they are expected to work as many hours as necessary to perform the duties of their positions. Overtime Compensation—Standard Work Schedules Pechanga Resort and Casino (“PR&C”) will compensate its non-exempt Team Members on a standard work schedule (“SWS”) for hours worked in excess of their regularly scheduled work hours as described below. For purposes of this policy, an SWS means a regular workweek during which a Team Member is not scheduled to work more than eight (8) hours on any workday within a 40-hour workweek. PR&C’s workweek begins at 12:00 a.m. on Monday and ends on Sunday at 11:59 p.m. Weekly Overtime:

For purposes of this policy, an SWS means a regular workweek during which a Team Member is not scheduled to work more than eight (8) hours on any workday within a 40-hour workweek. PR&C’s workweek begins at 12:00 a.m. on Monday and ends on Sunday at 11:59 p.m. Weekly Overtime: Except as provided in Section B.2.b, Team Members on an SWS will be paid overtime at the rate of one and a half times the Team Member’s regular rate of pay for any work performed in excess of 40 hours in a workweek. Daily Overtime: The first eight (8) hours worked on the the seventh consecutive day of work in a workweek. Hours worked in excess of eight (8) hours on the the seventh consecutive day of work in a workweek. Alternative Workweek Schedules Policy

The first eight (8) hours worked on the the seventh consecutive day of work in a workweek. Hours worked in excess of eight (8) hours on the the seventh consecutive day of work in a workweek. Alternative Workweek Schedules Policy Team Members on an SWS will be paid daily overtime at the rate of one and a half times the Team Member’s regular rate of pay for: Hours worked in excess of eight (8) hours, up to and including 12 hours, on a regularly scheduled workday of eight (8) hours or less; Team Members on an SWS will be paid daily overtime at the rate of double the Team Member’s regular rate of pay for: Hours worked in excess of 12 on a regularly scheduled workday of eight (8) hours or less; PR&C permits the adoption of an alternative workweek schedule (“AWS”) as specified below.

Team Members on an SWS will be paid daily overtime at the rate of double the Team Member’s regular rate of pay for: Hours worked in excess of 12 on a regularly scheduled workday of eight (8) hours or less; PR&C permits the adoption of an alternative workweek schedule (“AWS”) as specified below. For purposes of this policy, an AWS means a regular workweek schedule that requires a Team Member to work more than eight (8) hours on any workday within a 40-hour workweek. PR&C’s workweek begins at 12:00 a.m. on Monday and ends on Sunday at 11:59 p.m. 1.	Process a. An AWS may be adopted if, pursuant to a secret ballot election, at least two-thirds of the Team Members of the work unit vote in favor of it.

1.	Process a. An AWS may be adopted if, pursuant to a secret ballot election, at least two-thirds of the Team Members of the work unit vote in favor of it. For purposes of this policy, a “work unit” means a readily identifiable division, department, job classification, shift, separate physical location, or recognized subdivision of any such work unit, as designated by PR&C in its discretion. b. Prior to conducting a secret ballot election regarding an AWS, PR&C will hold one or more meetings with the Team Members of the affected work unit to discuss the proposed AWS; and provide the Team Members of the affected work unit a description of the effects, if any, of the proposed AWS on the Team Members’ wages, hours, and benefits. c.

b. Prior to conducting a secret ballot election regarding an AWS, PR&C will hold one or more meetings with the Team Members of the affected work unit to discuss the proposed AWS; and provide the Team Members of the affected work unit a description of the effects, if any, of the proposed AWS on the Team Members’ wages, hours, and benefits. c. Within 14 days following the last meeting held to discuss the AWS, the Team Members of the work unit will be permitted to vote on adopting the proposed AWS through a secret ballot election. The election will be held during regular working hours at the Team Members’ work site. PR&C will adopt the AWS if at least two-thirds of the Team Members in the work unit vote in favor of its adoption.

Within 14 days following the last meeting held to discuss the AWS, the Team Members of the work unit will be permitted to vote on adopting the proposed AWS through a secret ballot election. The election will be held during regular working hours at the Team Members’ work site. PR&C will adopt the AWS if at least two-thirds of the Team Members in the work unit vote in favor of its adoption. Any changes to current work schedules resulting from the secret ballot election will be implemented within a reasonable time following the election. 2.	Repealing the Alternative Workweek Schedule The AWS may be repealed by the Team Members of the work unit after the AWS has been in effect for at least one year and PR&C receives a written petition to repeal the AWS signed by at least one-third of the Team Members in the work unit.

Any changes to current work schedules resulting from the secret ballot election will be implemented within a reasonable time following the election. 2.	Repealing the Alternative Workweek Schedule The AWS may be repealed by the Team Members of the work unit after the AWS has been in effect for at least one year and PR&C receives a written petition to repeal the AWS signed by at least one-third of the Team Members in the work unit. A two-thirds vote of the Team Members is then required to repeal the AWS pursuant to a new secret ballot election. If the AWS is repealed, the work unit will revert to a non-AWS within 60 days. For business needs, PR&C also reserves the right to repeal the AWS and revert the work unit to a non-AWS at any time upon notice 60 days’ notice to the Team Members of the work unit.

A two-thirds vote of the Team Members is then required to repeal the AWS pursuant to a new secret ballot election. If the AWS is repealed, the work unit will revert to a non-AWS within 60 days. For business needs, PR&C also reserves the right to repeal the AWS and revert the work unit to a non-AWS at any time upon notice 60 days’ notice to the Team Members of the work unit. Overtime Compensation—Alternative Work Schedules PR&C will compensate its non-exempt Team Members on an AWS for hours worked in excess of their regularly scheduled work hours as described below. Weekly Overtime: Except as provided in Section D.2.c, Team Members on an AWS will be paid overtime at the rate of one and a half times the Team Member’s regular rate of pay for any work performed in excess of 40 hours in a workweek. Daily Overtime:

Weekly Overtime: Except as provided in Section D.2.c, Team Members on an AWS will be paid overtime at the rate of one and a half times the Team Member’s regular rate of pay for any work performed in excess of 40 hours in a workweek. Daily Overtime: Team Members on an AWS will be paid daily overtime at the rate of one and a half times the Team Member’s regular rate of pay for: The first three (3) hours worked in excess of a regularly scheduled workday of nine (9) hours; The first two (2) hours worked in excess of a regularly scheduled workday of 10 hours or more; and The first eight (8) hours worked on the second, third, and fourth consecutive workdays following the last regularly scheduled workday in the workweek. Hours worked in excess of three (3) beyond a regularly scheduled workday of nine (9) hours;

The first two (2) hours worked in excess of a regularly scheduled workday of 10 hours or more; and The first eight (8) hours worked on the second, third, and fourth consecutive workdays following the last regularly scheduled workday in the workweek. Hours worked in excess of three (3) beyond a regularly scheduled workday of nine (9) hours; Hours worked in excess of two (2) beyond a regularly scheduled workday of 10 hours or more; and Hours worked in excess of eight (8) hours on the second, third, and fourth consecutive workdays following the last regularly scheduled workday in a workweek. Team Members on an AWS will not be paid daily overtime for any regularly scheduled hours worked on any workday in a workweek. Hours worked in excess of eight (8) hours, up to and including 12 hours, on a regularly scheduled workday of eight (8) hours or less;

Hours worked in excess of eight (8) hours on the second, third, and fourth consecutive workdays following the last regularly scheduled workday in a workweek. Team Members on an AWS will not be paid daily overtime for any regularly scheduled hours worked on any workday in a workweek. Hours worked in excess of eight (8) hours, up to and including 12 hours, on a regularly scheduled workday of eight (8) hours or less; Team Members on an AWS will be paid daily overtime at the rate of double the Team Member’s regular rate of pay for: Hours worked in excess of 12 on a regularly scheduled workday of eight (8) hours or less;
Original Issue Date :	7/3/2013 SOP Title :	PRC Tribal Development Program Affected Departments : 	PRC ALL

Policy and Purpose :	This policy/program is developed to formalize a procedure by which Pechanga Tribal Members employed at PRC have the opportunity to indicate a career objective and have the organization provide the training and/or exposure required to achieve that objective. (Please type your change comments or explanations of the new policy below). Procedures: Within 6 months revised to Within 3 months Added to the policy: Once a plan has been put in place, a meeting with the PDC Board, tribal member employee and VP of the corresponding department and HR/Talent Management is to meet in order to review the plan . Procedure(s): Within 3 months of a Tribal Member obtaining a full time position at PRC, a meeting will be convened to discuss the member’s interest, career goals, and if appropriate, required training and/or education requirements.

. Procedure(s): Within 3 months of a Tribal Member obtaining a full time position at PRC, a meeting will be convened to discuss the member’s interest, career goals, and if appropriate, required training and/or education requirements. The meeting will be conducted by the Vice President of the division where the member is employed and the Vice President of HR/Talent Management. The outcome of the meeting(s) will be a formal organized plan to assist the member in achieving their career objective. The plan may include assessments, job rotations, attendance at conferences, internal/external training, more formalized education, and reports as necessary to gauge progress towards the goal. Also included will be regular (at least quarterly) follow-up meetings to correct as necessary. It is also possible that a member may be content in their current role and desire no future career growth. That will be noted and documented.

Also included will be regular (at least quarterly) follow-up meetings to correct as necessary. It is also possible that a member may be content in their current role and desire no future career growth. That will be noted and documented. Once a plan has been put in place, a meeting with the PDC Board, tribal member employee and VP of the corresponding department and HR/Talent Management is to meet in order to review the plan.
Original Issue Date :	      June 14, 2010 SOP Title :	Drug and Alcohol-Free Workplace Affected Departments : PRC ALL
source: PRC Drug  Alcohol Free Workplace Policy May 2025.docx

text: Policy and Purpose :	To establish Pechanga Resort Casino’s policy of maintaining a drug and alcohol-free workplace and set forth procedures for preventing and addressing violations of the policy. Included provisions that tests may be ordered by an authorized entity Included provisions if a supervisor or DPS personnel has reasonable suspicion Included provisions that drug testing may be administered by DPS or by an alternative designee POLICY It is the policy of PRC to maintain a drug and alcohol-free workplace. Therefore, PRC strictly prohibits: Using, possessing, purchasing, selling, trading, offering for sale or trade, conveying, manufacturing, distributing, transporting, or being Under the Influence of Illegal Drugs; Using a Legal Drug for any purpose other than the purpose for which it was prescribed or manufactured; or in a quantity, frequency, or manner contrary to the instructions or recommendations of the prescribing physician or manufacturer;
source: PRC Drug  Alcohol Free Workplace Policy May 2025.docx

text: Therefore, PRC strictly prohibits: Using, possessing, purchasing, selling, trading, offering for sale or trade, conveying, manufacturing, distributing, transporting, or being Under the Influence of Illegal Drugs; Using a Legal Drug for any purpose other than the purpose for which it was prescribed or manufactured; or in a quantity, frequency, or manner contrary to the instructions or recommendations of the prescribing physician or manufacturer; Violating any applicable law related to drugs or alcohol; Working while Under the Influence of alcohol or any Illegal Drug; Working while impaired by the use of a Legal Drug, whenever such impairment might:  (i) endanger the Team Member’s own safety or the safety of others; (ii) pose a risk of significant damage to PRC property or equipment; or (iii) substantially interfere with the Team Member’s job performance or the efficient operation of PRC’s business or equipment;
source: PRC Drug  Alcohol Free Workplace Policy May 2025.docx

DEFINITIONS
source: PRC Drug  Alcohol Free Workplace Policy May 2025.docx

text: Submitting an adulterated or substituted alcohol or drug testing sample; or Failing an alcohol or drug test ordered by PRC or an authorized entity. DEFINITIONS “Illegal Drug” means any drug that is not legally obtainable or is legally obtainable but has not been legally obtained. Marijuana/Cannabinoids are considered Illegal Drugs under federal law and this policy, regardless of any state law to the contrary. “Legal Drug” means any drug, including any prescription drug or over-the-counter drug, that has been legally obtained and is being used in a lawful manner for the purpose for which it was prescribed and manufactured. Marijuana/Cannabinoids are not considered Legal Drugs under federal law or this policy, regardless of any prescription, and regardless of any state law to the contrary. “Possession” means to have on one’s person, in one’s personal effects, in one’s vehicle or otherwise under one’s care, custody, or control.
source: PRC Drug  Alcohol Free Workplace Policy May 2025.docx

text: “Legal Drug” means any drug, including any prescription drug or over-the-counter drug, that has been legally obtained and is being used in a lawful manner for the purpose for which it was prescribed and manufactured. Marijuana/Cannabinoids are not considered Legal Drugs under federal law or this policy, regardless of any prescription, and regardless of any state law to the contrary. “Possession” means to have on one’s person, in one’s personal effects, in one’s vehicle or otherwise under one’s care, custody, or control. “Premises” means all property, facilities, buildings, structures, installations, work locations, work areas, or vehicles owned, operated, leased, or under the control of PRC to which such premises or property pertains. Private vehicles parked on premises or properties are also included under this definition.
source: PRC Drug  Alcohol Free Workplace Policy May 2025.docx

Private vehicles parked on premises or properties are also included under this definition. “Reasonable Suspicion” means a belief based on objective facts that would suggest to a reasonable person that the Team Member is Under the Influence in violation of this policy. Some examples of facts that would support a reasonable suspicion include evidence of drugs or alcohol on or around the Team Member, as well as: Physical signs
source: PRC Drug  Alcohol Free Workplace Policy May 2025.docx

text: “Reasonable Suspicion” means a belief based on objective facts that would suggest to a reasonable person that the Team Member is Under the Influence in violation of this policy. Some examples of facts that would support a reasonable suspicion include evidence of drugs or alcohol on or around the Team Member, as well as: Physical signs such as bloodshot eyes, dilated pupils, slurred speech, unsteady walking, uncoordinated movements, shakes or tremors, unexplained sweating or shivering, fidgeting, inability to sit still, sleeping at work or difficulty staying awake, unusual body or breath odor, or deterioration in appearance/grooming; Behavioral signs such as attendance problems (tardiness, pattern of absences, excessive absenteeism, etc.), declining performance or productivity, secretive behavior, withdrawal from others, or money problems; and Psychological signs
source: PRC Drug  Alcohol Free Workplace Policy May 2025.docx

text: Behavioral signs such as attendance problems (tardiness, pattern of absences, excessive absenteeism, etc.), declining performance or productivity, secretive behavior, withdrawal from others, or money problems; and Psychological signs such as unexplained changes in personality or attitude, sudden mood changes, irritability, angry outbursts, inappropriate laughing, unexplained fear or paranoia, or an inability to focus or concentrate. “Under the Influence” means having any measurable amount of the tested substance in the individual’s body, regardless of when or where it may have been consumed. NOTIFICATION OF CONVICTIONS, VIOLATIONS, AND DISPOSITIONS 1.	Criminal Drug Statute Violations Occurring in The Workplace. Any Team Member convicted of a criminal drug statute violation occurring in the workplace must notify the Human Resources Department in writing within five (5) calendar days of the conviction.
source: PRC Drug  Alcohol Free Workplace Policy May 2025.docx

text: NOTIFICATION OF CONVICTIONS, VIOLATIONS, AND DISPOSITIONS 1.	Criminal Drug Statute Violations Occurring in The Workplace. Any Team Member convicted of a criminal drug statute violation occurring in the workplace must notify the Human Resources Department in writing within five (5) calendar days of the conviction. PRC will take appropriate action, up to and including termination of employment or direction to participate in a rehabilitation program, within thirty (30) days of notification. PRC will make any required notifications within ten (10) days after receiving notice that a Team Member has been convicted of a criminal drug statute violation in the workplace. 2.	Other Criminal Drug-Related or Alcohol-Related Statute Violations and Offenses. Any Team Member violating a criminal drug-related or alcohol-related statute, or otherwise found by a court to have committed a drug-related or alcohol-related offense (including pleas of guilty and nolo contendre
source: PRC Drug  Alcohol Free Workplace Policy May 2025.docx

text: 2.	Other Criminal Drug-Related or Alcohol-Related Statute Violations and Offenses. Any Team Member violating a criminal drug-related or alcohol-related statute, or otherwise found by a court to have committed a drug-related or alcohol-related offense (including pleas of guilty and nolo contendre , deferred adjudication, probation and suspended sentences, or any similar disposition), in or outside of the workplace, must inform their supervisor and the Human Resources Department within five (5) calendar days of the violation or court disposition. Such violations and dispositions may lead to disciplinary action, up to and including separation from employment, or direction to participate in a rehabilitation program, if appropriate. TYPES OF TESTING PRC reserves the right to: Drug test all applicants prior to employment; Drug test all Team Members on a random basis without prior notification; Drug and/or alcohol test all Team Members for Reasonable Suspicion;
source: PRC Drug  Alcohol Free Workplace Policy May 2025.docx

E. TESTING PROCEDURE 1. General Provisions .
source: PRC Drug  Alcohol Free Workplace Policy May 2025.docx

text: 1. General Provisions . a.	Drug tests will measure the amount of commonly abused controlled substances in the Team Member’s body, including: Amphetamines, Opiates and Extended Opiates, Cocaine, Methamphetamines, and Phencyclidine (PCP), and in some instances Marijuana/Cannabinoids (See Sections E.3-4, below). b.	Alcohol tests will measure the Team Member’s blood alcohol content. c. All testing of current Team Members will be conducted without cost to the Team Member during the Team Member’s scheduled shift, and the Team Member will receive their regular compensation for the time they spend in testing. d.	All testing will be performed by qualified personnel according to scientifically acceptable methods. Collection of samples will be performed under reasonable and sanitary conditions, and PRC will maintain a record of the chain of custody of each sample.
source: PRC Drug  Alcohol Free Workplace Policy May 2025.docx

text: All testing of current Team Members will be conducted without cost to the Team Member during the Team Member’s scheduled shift, and the Team Member will receive their regular compensation for the time they spend in testing. d.	All testing will be performed by qualified personnel according to scientifically acceptable methods. Collection of samples will be performed under reasonable and sanitary conditions, and PRC will maintain a record of the chain of custody of each sample. e.	Prior to testing, Team Members will have the opportunity to inform the testing personnel of any prescription or non-prescription drugs they have taken that may affect the outcome of the test. f.	Team Members may be placed on paid administrative leave pending the results of any drug or alcohol test. g.	If the test results are negative, the Team Member will be returned to work.

If the test results are positive, the test sample will be sent to a licensed medical facility for final verification. If the final verification confirms the positive result, the Team Member will be found to have violated this policy. i.	Team Members who have successfully completed their introductory period may challenge results of a positive drug test. In such a situation, PRC will retest the original sample for accuracy.

Pre-Employment Drug Testing . Drug testing of all applicants will be conducted pursuant to the Pre-Employment Drug Testing Policy. 3. Reasonable Suspicion Testing . a.	PRC will order a Team Member to submit to reasonable suspicion drug or alcohol testing if a supervisor or security personnel has a Reasonable Suspicion, confirmed by a DPS Manager, that the Team Member is Under the Influence in violation of this policy.

When a Team Member is ordered to submit to reasonable suspicion testing, the facts supporting the Reasonable Suspicion will be documented and explained to the Team Member. c.	Team Members ordered to submit to reasonable suspicion testing will be placed on paid administrative leave pending the test results. 4. Post-Accident Testing . If supervisor or security personnel has a Reasonable Suspicion, confirmed by a DPS Manager, to believe a Team Member has caused an on-the-job injury that is considered recordable under OSHA guidelines ( i.e.
source: PRC Drug  Alcohol Free Workplace Policy May 2025.docx

Refusal to submit to the substance test shall be grounds for immediate separation from employment. 5. Random Drug Testing . Each month, Team Members in the safety-sensitive or security-sensitive departments listed on Attachment A will be selected at random for monthly drug testing using a computer-based software specifically designed to conduct random drug testing.

Team Members selected for random drug testing will be tested for their use of commonly abused controlled substances, including: Amphetamines, Opiates and Extended Opiates, Cocaine, Methamphetamines, and Phencyclidine (PCP). Marijuana/Cannabinoids will not be included in the random drug testing panel, but will continue to be included in the Reasonable Suspicion and post-accident drug testing panels. c. text: After Hours Drug and Alcohol Testing . After hours drug or alcohol testing of a Team Member may be initiated if supervisor or security personnel has a Reasonable Suspicion, confirmed by a DPS Manager, that the Team Member is Under the Influence. The Department Manager or authorized supervisor must make the decision to test the Team Member, and both managers must fill out the appropriate request form to move forward with testing. Once the decision to test the Team Member has been made, Department Management must contact DPS via x2610 to request drug or alcohol testing and submit the completed request form. Department Management must also immediately notify Senior Management and HR Operations that after-hours testing will occur. DPS or PRC’s alternative designee will administer the test and coordinate the secure transmission of any testing specimen to the applicable laboratory.

COUNSELING/TEAM MEMBER ASSISTANCE a.	Team Members who suspect they may be developing a problem with either drugs or alcohol should bring their concerns to their supervisor or Human Resources. However, the decision to seek diagnosis and accept treatment for alcohol or drug abuse or addiction is primarily the individual Team Member’s responsibility. Likewise, although the costs of treatment may be covered by the Team Member’s benefit plan in some instances, the ultimate financial responsibility for treatment belongs to the Team Member.

H. USE OF LEGAL DRUGS Nothing in this Policy is intended to prohibit the lawful, customary, and appropriate purchase, use, or possession of Legal Drugs.
